_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (SklepDemo/NotkaApp). Output interakcji jest prerenderowany (nie z żywego modelu). ZERO PII.
_meta.note: Egzemplarz referencyjny typu tune: parametry → autorskie efekty (dane) + scorowany checkpoint.

intro: The same inputs and the same model can produce different answers, because generation is steered by parameters. In QA, reproducible results are the foundation of trustworthy documentation and regression testing — uncontrolled parameters yield unpredictable output.

blocks.text: Three controls shape the answer: temperature and top-p (randomness) plus max tokens (length). Before you trust output in test documentation, you need to know how to set them.

blocks.title: Apparent determinism
blocks.text: A low temperature reduces randomness, but it does not guarantee identical results on every run — this is still a probabilistic model.
the control for randomness when selecting the next token.

items: Low (~0.0–0.3): conservative, reproducible, formal answers — for documentation and test steps.
High (~0.8–1.2): varied, creative, less predictable answers — for brainstorming test data.

blocks.text: High temperature in QA means a lack of reproducibility (two runs = two different steps), content drift, difficulty in review and audit, and the risk of silent changes during regression.

blocks.title: Rule
blocks.text: When reproducibility and compliance with a requirement matter — lower the randomness.
a cumulative-probability threshold — it narrows the pool of next-token candidates to the most probable ones that sum to p.
Both parameters control variety; you usually adjust one at a time. Low top-p = a narrower, safer choice (a stable vocabulary of QA terms); high = broader, riskier phrasing.

screens.title: max tokens — when the answer gets cut off

blocks.original: maximum number of tokens in the answer
blocks.text: a hard limit on output length.

Exceeding the limit cuts the answer off mid-sentence, mid-table, or mid-list — the final steps or acceptance criteria are missing. You can recognize it by a truncated sentence and an unclosed list. The fix: raise the limit or ask for a shorter format.
Compare two presets for the same task (writing out the steps of a test case). Variant A: temp 0.2 / top-p 0.3 / high limit. Variant B: temp 1.0 / top-p 0.95 / low limit.
For documentation, choose lower randomness and headroom for length — this gives reproducibility and no truncation. In a moment you will check this with the sliders.

interaction.title: Generation sliders
interaction.intro: Set the parameters and see how the simulated output changes for the task "write out the login test steps in SklepDemo". Then answer the checkpoint.
interaction.outputLabel: Simulated output for the selected settings:

levels.label: Low (0.2)
levels.effect: Formality: high. Test steps are reproducible, with the same layout on every run.

levels.label: High (1.0)
levels.effect: Formality: low. Steps differ between runs — no reproducibility, risk of content drift.

levels.label: Low (0.3)
levels.effect: The vocabulary of QA terms is stable and predictable.

levels.label: High (0.95)
levels.effect: Broader, sometimes risky phrasing — harder to review.

levels.label: High limit (512)
levels.effect: Completeness: full. All steps and acceptance criteria fit.

levels.label: Low limit (64)
levels.effect: ⚠ Answer cut off — the list of steps breaks off halfway, no summary. Raise the limit.

checkpoint.prompt: For writing out the steps of a test case (where reproducibility and completeness matter), which setting is safer?

options.text: temp 0.2 / top-p 0.3 / high limit — low randomness and length headroom
temp 1.0 / top-p 0.95 / low limit — creative and short

checkpoint.feedbackCorrect: Correct: low randomness gives reproducibility, and a high limit protects against truncating the list of steps.
checkpoint.feedbackIncorrect: High randomness ruins reproducibility, and a low limit cuts off the steps — bad for documentation. Choose low temperature and length headroom.

points: Temperature and top-p control randomness; max tokens controls the length of the answer.
For QA, prefer low randomness and length headroom — reproducibility and no truncation.
Always verify the reproducibility and completeness of the output before using it in documentation.
checklist: Did I lower the randomness where reproducibility matters?
Will the length limit fit the entire answer?
Is the output the same on a re-run?
summary.nextStep: Next step: M4 — embeddings and semantic similarity (or the next mandatory module on your path).
